description: This figure plots the variation of yield stress and ductility as a function of aluminum content for polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys in the as-cast or annealed condition. The x-axis shows aluminum content from 0 to about 40%, the left y-axis denotes yield stress in MPa (solid triangles, solid line), and the right y-axis denotes ductility in percent (solid circles, dashed line). Yield stress rises steadily with increasing Al content, reaching a maximum near 25% Al at roughly 650 MPa, followed by a sharp decrease between 25% and 28% Al and a relatively flat or weakly increasing trend at higher Al contents. Ductility shows a broadly inverse relationship, falling rapidly with increasing Al content up to 25% Al, but exhibits a small local peak near 28% Al before continuing to decline toward 40% Al. The caption states that data are compiled from Refs. [2,5,8] and the present study. These trends support the interpretation that solution and short-range-order hardening dominate strengthening up to 25% Al, while the small ductility recovery near 28% Al correlates with a peak in work-hardening rate over a composition range where flow stress is relatively constant, consistent with the Considere criterion discussed in the text.

Figure 2 plots the variation of work hardening rate (WHR), measured between 1 and 1.5% plastic strain, as a function of aluminum content for polycrystalline as-cast or annealed Fe-Al alloys. The chart contains two data series: solid circles denoting binary Fe-Al alloys and dashed squares denoting Fe-Al-B alloys containing about 0.1% B. The WHR remains low (roughly 1–2 GPa) for disordered alloys with less than ~20% Al, increases steeply through the 20–28% Al range, reaches a sharp maximum of approximately 16 GPa at 28–29% Al, and then declines to moderate values above ~35% Al. The Fe-Al-B data points indicate only a slight increase relative to the B-free samples, with little consistent change in the work hardening rate. This composition dependence supports the conclusion that the ordered state exhibits significantly higher work hardening than the disordered state, with the pronounced peak near 28–29% Al reflecting superdislocation dipole hardening mechanisms and a high density of straight screw-oriented superdislocations in the ordered DO3/B2 structures.

"Fault energies in Fe–Al alloys" presents the variation of antiphase boundary (APB) energies as a function of aluminum content (20–45%) in Fe–Al alloys with different long-range ordered states. The plot contains three distinct data series: solid circles connected by a solid line denote the APBI energy in B2-ordered alloys, which increases steeply from roughly 15 mJ/m² at ~27% Al to about 110 mJ/m² at ~36% Al; solid squares connected by a short-dashed line represent the APBI energy in DO₃-ordered alloys near 25–30% Al, showing modest variation in the range of roughly 65–80 mJ/m²; and open triangles connected by a long-dashed line show the APBII energy in DO₃-ordered alloys in the same composition window, decreasing from about 85 mJ/m² at ~26% Al to about 65 mJ/m² at ~29% Al. The caption states that these data are compiled from literature sources [19–21]. The key contrast is that B2-ordered APBI energies rise dramatically with Al content, whereas DO₃-ordered APBI and APBII energies remain comparatively low and are either weakly varying or decreasing. This figure supports the core conclusion that low APB energies in the DO₃-ordered, low-Al regime cannot keep partial dislocations closely coupled as superdislocations, whereas the high APBI energies in B2-ordered, high-Al alloys favor tightly bound dislocation pairs, directly governing the dislocation configurations and work-hardening response discussed in the text.

Fig. 4 presents the composition dependence of elastic stiffness constants and the Zener anisotropy factor for Fe-Al alloys, with data taken from Refs. [22,23]. The graph plots C44 (filled circles, solid line), (C11 - C12)/2 (filled squares, solid line), and the anisotropy factor A = 2C44/(C11 - C12) (open triangles, dashed line) against aluminium content from 0 to 40% on the horizontal axis. Stiffness constants are read on the left vertical axis (GPa), while the anisotropy factor uses the right vertical axis. C44 increases moderately from about 118 GPa to a peak near 130 GPa around 30% Al before slightly decreasing. In contrast, (C11 - C12)/2 decreases from approximately 47 GPa to a minimum near 20 GPa in the 25-30% Al range, then rises again toward 33 GPa at 40% Al. Consequently, the anisotropy factor A rises sharply from about 2.5 to a pronounced maximum near 6.5-7.0 around 25-30% Al, attributed to the simultaneous increase in C44 and drop in (C11 - C12)/2, and then falls to about 4.5 at 40% Al. The figure supports the paper's argument that Fe3Al alloys exhibit extremely high elastic anisotropy that coincides with the onset of long-range order and the transition from single dislocations to superdislocations in the 25-30% Al composition range, which is linked to the anomalous work hardening behaviour.

This transmission electron micrograph (Fig. 5) shows single dislocations arranged in a loose cellular distribution in a disordered Fe–15% Al alloy after room-temperature compression to 2–3% plastic strain at a nominal strain rate of 3×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹. The image, taken with the zone axis (stress axis) close to [0 1 3] and the imaging g vector (arrowed) as 2 0 0, reveals an incipient cell structure formed by weak clustering of single dislocations in thicker foil regions, with many dislocation segments in the clustered regions identified as dipoles. The micrograph demonstrates that this lower-Al-content, disordered material deforms by the movement of individual (1/2)<111> dislocations rather than superdislocations. This dislocation configuration serves as the baseline structure for comparing the transition to superdislocation-mediated deformation and associated work-hardening peaks observed at higher Al contents (near 28% Al) in the study.

The figure presents a pair of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs showing curled single dislocations in a disordered or short-range ordered Fe–20% Al alloy after room-temperature compression to 2–3% plastic strain. The zone axis (stress axis) is [0 1 1] and the imaging vector, indicated by arrows, is parallel to 011. The two micrographs were recorded using opposite-sign imaging vectors (±g), and the relative displacement of dislocation images between them confirms the dipole arrangement of many dislocation segments and small loops, explicitly marked as "d" in the second micrograph. The single dislocations have a Burgers vector of (1/2)<111> and appear generally smoothly curved, with some preference for screw orientations. The dislocation dipoles and small loops are produced by trailing from gliding screw dislocations. The second micrograph includes a scale bar of 0.2 μm. Collectively, these images support the conclusion that in disordered or weakly ordered Fe–Al alloys, deformation occurs by the motion of individual (1/2)<111> dislocations that organize into dipolar configurations rather than well-developed cell structures, providing direct TEM evidence for dipole identification through contrast reversal under opposite g-vectors.

This transmission electron micrograph shows the dislocation substructure in a Fe–20% Al alloy after room-temperature compression to 2–3% plastic strain. The image reveals straight screw dislocations, some loosely paired, along with edge dipole segments and small dislocation loops distributed across the thin foil. The micrograph was recorded near the [0 0 1] zone axis with an imaging g vector of 1 1 0, as indicated by the arrow, and the scale bar represents 0.2 μm. According to the caption and text, these dislocations have a Burgers vector of (1/2)<1 1 1> and represent single dislocations characteristic of disordered or short-range ordered Fe–Al alloys containing 15–20% Al. The narrow edge dipoles and small loops are reported to be produced by trailing from gliding screw dislocations. This figure supports the paper's argument that low-Al-content Fe–Al alloys deform by individual dislocation motion, contrasting with the superdislocation-mediated deformation observed in higher-Al ordered alloys, thereby contributing to the understanding of composition-dependent work hardening mechanisms.

This figure presents paired transmission electron micrographs of deformed Fe–25% Al alloy examined along a zone axis near [0 0 1] with the imaging g vector parallel to [1 0 0]. The left dark-field image, acquired using the 1 0 0 beam at the Bragg condition, reveals curved antiphase boundaries (APBs) and dislocations, where thermal APBs from coarse B2 domains are marked “t” and deformation-induced APBI faults produced by (1/2)[1 1 1] dislocation motion are marked “s”. The right weak-beam image, taken with the 2 0 0 beam under approximately 4 0 0 beam excitation, resolves fine dislocation contrast together with residual APB contrast, showing loosely coupled dislocation pairs labeled “s”. Both images demonstrate that in this DO3-ordered alloy the APBI and APBII energies are too low to sustain closely coupled superdislocations; instead, dislocations glide largely as individual (1/2)[1 1 1] units or as loose pairs constituting B2 or DO3 superdislocations. The micrographs thus provide direct evidence that weak APB coupling controls the deformation microstructure and supports the interpretation of modest work hardening in this composition regime.

Bright field transmission electron micrograph of deformed Fe–25% Al alloy showing a high density of near-screw dislocations arranged as loosely coupled pairs (labeled p) and dipoles (labeled d). The sample was deformed by room-temperature compression to 2–3% plastic strain at a strain rate of 3×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ and exhibits DO₃ long-range order with coarse B2 domains developed during slow cooling after casting. The image was recorded near the [0 0 1] zone axis using a [1 1 0] imaging vector (arrowed) with a scale bar of 0.2 μm. The micrograph reveals that antiphase boundary (APB) energies are insufficiently high to maintain closely coupled superdislocations; instead, dislocations glide largely as individual (1/2)⟨111⟩ dislocations, leaving edge dipole segments and loosely coupled screw dipole configurations. This dislocation arrangement supports the interpretation that the relatively low APB energies in imperfectly ordered DO₃ Fe–25% Al lead to weakly coupled dislocations that behave independently during glide, contributing to the observed work hardening behavior.

This bright-field TEM micrograph displays a high density of superdislocations in deformed Fe–28% Al, the composition corresponding to the peak in the work-hardening curve. The dislocations appear predominantly as long, straight segments oriented in near-screw directions along their {1 1 0} glide planes, visible throughout the field of view with a scale bar of 0.5 μm. Caption and accompanying text state that these are superdislocations composed of two (1/2)<1 1 1> partials, which are perfect superdislocations for the B2 lattice but imperfect for the D O 3 lattice, trailing APBII faults as they glide without APBI faults. The image reveals a relatively uniform distribution of these straight superdislocations bearing several different Burgers vectors, often interacting to form networks and dipole configurations. This morphology—straight screw-oriented superdislocations—is central to the paper's argument that strong Peierls forces and superdislocation dipole locking are responsible for the anomalously high work-hardening rate at this aluminum content.

Figure 11 presents a pair of bright-field TEM micrographs of deformed Fe–28% Al (the composition corresponding to the peak in the work-hardening curve) imaged under +g and −g conditions with the zone axis near [0 0 1] and the imaging g vector ±200 (arrowed). The two micrographs act as a comparative set: reversing the sign of g causes dipole pairs (marked d) to change their relative dislocation contrast and position, confirming their dipole character, while non-dipole pairs (marked p) show no such reversal, indicating identical Burgers vectors. The images reveal straight, near-screw superdislocations composed of two (1/2)<111> partials gliding on {110} planes and trailing APBII faults. Many superdislocations are arranged in dipole configurations (d), while other parallel pairs (p) are straight edge dislocations with identical Burgers vectors produced by reactions between glissile <111> superdislocations. These observations support the central conclusion that the anomalous work-hardening peak in Fe–28% Al arises from strong superdislocation dipole locks and sessile <100> edge dislocation locks on {001} planes that block further room-temperature superdislocation motion.

Figure 12 comprises two bright-field TEM micrographs of deformed Fe–28 at.% Al captured under +g and −g imaging conditions near the [0 0 1] zone axis with an imaging vector of type 1 1 0. The micrographs display straight and parallel reaction dislocations marked "r" within the DO3-ordered matrix, which caption and context identify as sessile edge <100> dislocation locks produced by reactions between glissile <111> superdislocations on different {110} planes. The contrast difference between the +g and −g pair helps confirm the line directions and crystallographic character of these reaction products. Dense dislocation networks and straight near-screw segments are visible throughout the field of view. The 0.2 μm scale bar indicates nanoscale defect separations. This figure provides direct microstructural evidence linking the formation of immobile <100> locks to the anomalously high work-hardening rate peak near Fe–28% Al.

This weak-beam transmission electron micrograph (imaging condition near g:3–4g, zone axis near [0 0 1], g vector 2 0 0 as arrowed) displays the dislocation substructure in room-temperature deformed Fe–28% Al, the alloy composition at the peak of the work-hardening curve. The main field of view together with two upper insets magnifies superdislocation reactions labeled “r,” showing straight segments produced by interacting glissile superdislocations. Caption and accompanying text state that these reactions generate sessile edge <100> dislocation locks via the interaction of two superdislocations with different <111> Burgers vectors on distinct {1 1 0} glide planes—exemplified by [1 1 1] + [1 1¯ 1] forming a [1 0 0] edge pair along [0 1 0] lying on a (0 0 1) plane. The 0.2 μm scale bar underscores the nanoscale defect distribution. These immobile locks obstruct further superdislocation glide, furnishing direct microstructural evidence that dislocation locking underlies the sharp work-hardening maximum near 28% Al.

Bright-field TEM micrograph of deformed Fe–30% Al (induction melted, drop cast, slow cooled) showing straight dislocation segments after room-temperature compression to 2–3% plastic strain. The zone axis is near [0 0 1] with the imaging vector (arrowed) as 110, and the scale bar denotes 0.2 μm. The image reveals numerous straight near-screw superdislocations and stepped structures resulting from dislocation reactions; the visible dislocation pairs arise from such reactions rather than from partials of glissile superdislocations. Because the APBI fault energy is now sufficiently high, the separation of the two (1/2)〈111〉 partials constituting the superdislocation is small. This micrograph supports the conclusion that deformation in higher-Al Fe–Al alloys occurs by movement of superdislocations, with reacted dislocations producing stepped configurations, contributing to the understanding of work-hardening mechanisms in weakly DO₃-ordered material.

Figure 14 presents paired bright-field transmission electron micrographs showing typical dislocation structures in deformed Fe–30% Al. The left panel (scale bar 0.25 μm) provides a low-magnification overview of the dense, intertwined dislocation network formed after room-temperature compression, while the right panel (scale bar 0.1 μm) offers higher magnification detail where paired dislocations marked "d" are identified as dipole segments and loops. The caption states the zone axis is near [1 1 1] with the imaging vector (arrowed) as 1 1 0. Citation text clarifies that deformation occurs by superdislocations composed of two (1/2)<1 1 1> partial dislocations, and because the APBI fault energy is now sufficiently high in this alloy, the separation of the partials is small. The parallel image pairs visible in the right micrograph correspond to short or long dipole dislocation pairs rather than glissile superdislocation partials. Additionally, superdislocations are often straight in screw orientations, with dislocation reactions producing stepped structures. These micrographs support the conclusion that work hardening in Fe–30% Al involves the motion of closely coupled superdislocations and the accumulation of edge dislocation dipoles and loops, consistent with the high work-hardening rate observed in this composition range.

Figure 16 is a bright-field TEM micrograph of deformed Fe–30% Al alloy showing superdislocation segments and background fringes traversing the field of view. The sample was induction-melted, slow-cooled, and room-temperature compressed to 2–3% plastic strain. Imaging conditions are near the [0 0 1] zone axis with g = 1 1 0 (arrowed). The micrograph reveals B2 superdislocations composed of two closely coupled (1/2)⟨1 1 1⟩ partial dislocations, attributed to the higher APBI fault energy in this alloy. Additionally, numerous fringes are visible across the entire area, which represent residual APBII contrast arising from the destruction of weak DO₃ order by the moving partial pairs. The caption and citation text note that the visible dislocation pairs result from dislocation reactions rather than from glissile superdislocation partials, and that similar structures appear in Fe–35% Al. These observations support the conclusion that deformation in higher-Al Fe–Al alloys occurs by superdislocation motion that disrupts long-range order, producing characteristic APB fringe contrast and straight near-screw dislocation configurations.

Figure 17 presents a pair of bright-field transmission electron micrographs showing typical dislocation structures in Fe–35% Al alloy deformed at room temperature to 2–3% plastic strain. The left micrograph (scale bar 0.25 μm) reveals a high density of straight, near-screw superdislocations and numerous edge dipole segments distributed throughout the slowly-cooled, B2-ordered matrix. The right micrograph (scale bar 0.1 μm) provides a higher-magnification view of the region indicated by the arrow, highlighting edge dipole segments being dragged out of glissile screw superdislocations as they encounter obstacles. For both images, the zone axis is near [1 1 0] and the imaging vector is [1 1 0] (arrowed). The dislocations are B2 superdislocations, each composed of two (1/2)<1 1 1> partials lying close together due to the high APBI fault energy. The caption and text report that some remnant DO3 order is present, and the movement of these superdislocations destroys part of this order, leaving weak remnant fringes. These observations support the study’s conclusion that the dislocation substructures in higher-Al Fe–Al alloys are dominated by closely coupled superdislocations and their dipole debris, contributing to the alloy’s work-hardening behavior.

This figure presents a pair of bright-field transmission electron micrographs of deformed Fe–35% Al alloy, captured under +g and −g imaging conditions (left and right halves, respectively) with an imaging vector of type [1 1 0] and zone axis near [1 1 3]. The micrographs reveal a high density of edge dipole segments, several of which are explicitly marked with "d". The images show superdislocations of the B2 lattice composed of two closely coupled (1/2)<111> partials, which appear as narrow pairs due to the high APBI fault energy. The paired imaging conditions allow unambiguous identification of the dipolar dislocation segments formed during room-temperature deformation. Contextual text notes that these superdislocations destroy some degree of DO₃ order during glide. The scale bar indicates 0.5 μm. Overall, the figure provides direct TEM evidence that the work-hardened microstructure of Fe–35% Al is characterized by abundant edge dipoles associated with screw superdislocations, supporting the mechanism of dislocation multiplication and dipole hardening in high-aluminum Fe–Al alloys.

This bright-field transmission electron micrograph (Fig. 19) displays the dislocation substructure in deformed Fe–35% Al alloy. The image reveals dislocation segments and faint background fringes distributed across the field of view, with a scale bar of 0.25 μm. The zone axis is near [1 1 3] and the imaging vector, indicated by the arrow, is [1 1 0]. Visible are relatively straight, near-screw dislocation segments together with numerous edge dipole segments, consistent with the B2-ordered state of this alloy. As stated in the caption and related text, the dislocations are B2 superdislocations composed of two closely coupled (1/2)[1 1 1] partials due to high APBI fault energy, and the weak remnant fringes reflect APBII faults produced as superdislocation motion destroys weak DO3 order. The sample was induction melted, drop cast, and slow cooled, then compressed at room temperature to 2–3% strain at 3×10^-4 s^-1. This figure supports the mechanism that work hardening in this alloy arises from closely coupled two-partial superdislocations and their interaction with residual DO3 order rather than from four-partial superdislocations.
